q: Which particle has the highest penetration power among alpha, beta, and gamma radiation?
exp: Gamma rays are electromagnetic waves with no mass or charge, so they penetrate most.

q: The charge of an alpha particle is:
exp: Alpha particle is He nucleus with 2 protons → charge = +2e.

q: In beta minus decay, a neutron transforms into:
exp: n → p + e⁻ + ν̄ shows conservation of charge and energy.

q: Which law is obeyed in radioactive decay?
exp: Radioactive decay follows N = N₀e^(-λt).

q: Half-life depends on:
exp: Half-life is intrinsic property of nucleus, unaffected by external conditions.

q: If decay constant increases, half-life:
exp: T½ = ln2/λ, so inverse relation.

q: The SI unit of radioactive activity is:
exp: 1 Bq = 1 decay per second.

q: 1 Curie is equal to:
exp: Curie is a large unit of activity used in older systems.

q: Which radiation is most affected by electric field?
exp: Beta particles are light and charged, so they deflect most.

q: Gamma rays are best described as:
exp: Gamma rays are high-energy photons emitted from nucleus.

q: In alpha decay, atomic number decreases by:
exp: Loss of 2 protons reduces Z by 2.

q: Mass number in alpha decay decreases by:
exp: Alpha particle removes 2 protons + 2 neutrons = 4 nucleons.

q: Which particle has no charge?
exp: Neutron is electrically neutral.

q: Binding energy is related to:
exp: Binding energy = Δm c².

q: Mass defect is:
opts: Difference between mass of nucleons and nucleus
exp: Part of mass converted to binding energy.

q: Binding energy per nucleon is maximum for:
exp: Iron nucleus is most stable.

q: Fission is:
exp: Heavy nucleus splits into lighter nuclei releasing energy.

q: Fusion occurs at:
exp: Fusion requires extremely high temperature to overcome repulsion.

q: Energy released in nuclear reactions is due to:
exp: E = mc² explains nuclear energy release.

q: Neutron was discovered by:
exp: James Chadwick discovered neutron in 1932.

q: Radioactivity is a:
exp: Radioactivity originates from unstable nuclei.

q: Which radiation has maximum ionizing power?
exp: Alpha particles strongly ionize matter due to large mass and charge.

q: Penetration power order is:
exp: Gamma penetrates most, alpha least.

q: Which particle is emitted in beta plus decay?
exp: β⁺ decay emits positron.

q: Decay constant represents:
opts: Probability of decay per unit time
exp: λ is probability per unit time of decay.

q: Activity of a sample decreases because:
exp: Activity A = λN, so depends on N.

q: Which is correct about gamma radiation?
opts: Has no mass and no charge
exp: Gamma rays are neutral photons.

q: In nuclear reaction, total number of nucleons is:
exp: Mass number is conserved in nuclear reactions.

q: Which nucleus is most stable?
exp: Iron-like nuclei have highest binding energy per nucleon.

q: Beta particles are identical to:
exp: β⁻ particles are electrons emitted from nucleus.

q: In radioactive decay, activity is proportional to:
exp: A = λN shows proportionality to N.

q: Half-life means:
opts: Time for half nuclei to decay
exp: Half-life is time for 50% decay.

q: Which is correct about nuclear force?
exp: Strong nuclear force acts at very short distances.

q: In fission, energy is released because:
exp: Binding energy difference releases energy.

q: Which radiation is stopped by paper?
exp: Alpha has very low penetration power.

q: Which radiation has highest range in air?
exp: Gamma rays travel long distances in air.

q: Radioactive decay follows:
exp: Decay is exponential in nature.

q: If half-life increases, decay is:
exp: Longer half-life means slower decay.

q: Which is correct?
exp: Electron carries negative charge.
